notes: Curated reference for FooLab's 'read more' E-number details. Concern levels follow the same low/medium/high rubric used elsewhere in the app. Not medical advice.

conditions.sulfite_sensitivity: People with asthma or sulfite sensitivity
conditions.ibs: People with IBS or gut sensitivity

additives.name: Curcumin
additives.what: Yellow-orange color extracted from turmeric root. A natural, plant-derived pigment.
additives.smallDose: Considered safe at the levels used in food. Widely consumed as turmeric in cooking.
additives.largeDose: No significant concerns at typical daily intakes. Very high concentrated supplements (grams per day) can irritate the gut.

additives.name: Tartrazine
additives.what: Synthetic yellow azo dye. One of the 'Southampton six' colorings linked to hyperactivity in susceptible children.
additives.smallDose: EFSA keeps it approved but EU law requires a warning label on foods that contain it.
additives.largeDose: Regular consumption may trigger hyperactivity in sensitive children and allergic-type reactions (hives, asthma) in a small fraction of adults.

additives.name: Quinoline Yellow
additives.what: Synthetic yellow dye. Banned in the US; one of the Southampton six.
additives.smallDose: Tolerated by most, but EU mandates a hyperactivity warning on labels.
additives.largeDose: Frequent consumption associated with attention and behaviour changes in sensitive children.

additives.name: Sunset Yellow FCF
additives.what: Synthetic orange-yellow azo dye. One of the Southampton six.
additives.smallDose: Low intake tolerated by most adults.
additives.largeDose: Linked to hyperactivity in sensitive children; some people report hives or stomach upset.

additives.name: Cochineal / Carminic acid
additives.what: Deep red color derived from dried cochineal insects.
additives.smallDose: Not vegetarian/vegan. Generally safe in typical food amounts.
additives.largeDose: Can trigger allergic reactions, including rare but serious anaphylaxis, in susceptible people.

additives.name: Azorubine / Carmoisine
additives.what: Synthetic red azo dye. Southampton six member.
additives.smallDose: Occasional intake generally tolerated.
additives.largeDose: Linked to hyperactivity in sensitive children and intolerance reactions in some adults.

additives.name: Ponceau 4R
additives.what: Synthetic red azo dye. Banned in the US. Southampton six.
additives.smallDose: Tolerated by most at low intakes.
additives.largeDose: Hyperactivity risk in sensitive children; rare allergic-type reactions.

additives.name: Allura Red AC
additives.what: Synthetic red azo dye. Southampton six.
additives.smallDose: Occasional intake generally tolerated.
additives.largeDose: Long-term frequent intake linked in recent studies to gut inflammation in animal models and behaviour effects in sensitive children.

additives.name: Chlorophyll
additives.what: Green pigment extracted from plants. Same compound that makes leaves green.
additives.smallDose: Considered safe.
additives.largeDose: No significant concerns at food-level intakes.

additives.name: Plain caramel
additives.what: Brown color made by heating sugars. The simplest caramel, no ammonia/sulfite process.
additives.smallDose: Considered safe.
additives.largeDose: Little concern beyond the sugar content.

additives.name: Sulfite ammonia caramel
additives.what: Darkest caramel color, made with ammonia and sulfites. Common in colas and soy-style sauces.
additives.smallDose: EFSA sets a daily limit. Occasional soda intake fits well within it.
additives.largeDose: Contains trace 4-MEI, classified as 'possibly carcinogenic' by IARC. Heavy daily cola drinkers can approach intake of concern.

additives.name: Beta-carotene
additives.what: Orange pigment naturally found in carrots, also a vitamin A precursor.
additives.smallDose: Safe and nutritionally useful.
additives.largeDose: Very high doses from supplements (not food additives) have been linked to lung cancer risk in smokers.

additives.name: Paprika extract
additives.what: Red-orange color from paprika peppers.
additives.smallDose: Considered safe.
additives.largeDose: No notable concern at food-level intakes.

additives.name: Beetroot red (betanin)
additives.what: Deep pink-red pigment from beetroot.
additives.smallDose: Safe. Can harmlessly turn urine pink.
additives.largeDose: No significant concerns.

additives.name: Anthocyanins
additives.what: Purple-red pigments from grapes, berries, red cabbage.
additives.smallDose: Safe; also antioxidant.
additives.largeDose: No notable concern at food-level intakes.

additives.name: Titanium dioxide
additives.what: Bright white pigment. Banned as a food additive in the EU (2022) over concerns it may damage DNA.
additives.smallDose: Still allowed in some non-EU markets. EFSA could no longer consider it safe after recent nanoparticle data.
additives.largeDose: Repeated ingestion may accumulate in the gut. Avoid if possible.

additives.name: Iron oxides
additives.what: Black, red, or yellow mineral pigments.
additives.smallDose: Considered safe.
additives.largeDose: Typical food use provides negligible iron relative to dietary needs.

additives.name: Sorbic acid
additives.what: A preservative that stops mold and yeast growth.
additives.smallDose: Considered safe; metabolised like fatty acids.
additives.largeDose: Some people report skin rash on direct contact, but dietary intake is generally well tolerated.

additives.name: Potassium sorbate
additives.what: Potassium salt of sorbic acid. Most common mold/yeast inhibitor.
additives.smallDose: Widely considered one of the safer preservatives.
additives.largeDose: Occasionally implicated in mild skin or respiratory reactions at high exposures.

additives.name: Benzoic acid
additives.what: Preservative active against yeasts and bacteria.
additives.smallDose: Approved but may cause pseudo-allergic reactions in some asthmatics or people with urticaria.
additives.largeDose: In acidic drinks containing vitamin C (E300), can form traces of benzene — manufacturers generally avoid this combination now.

additives.name: Sodium benzoate
additives.what: Sodium salt of benzoic acid. Common in soft drinks, sauces, pickles.
additives.smallDose: Approved; daily limits easily met at normal intakes.
additives.largeDose: Can react with vitamin C to form traces of benzene. Associated with hyperactivity in sensitive children when combined with artificial colors.

additives.name: Sulfur dioxide
additives.what: A gas-based preservative used in wine, dried fruit, fruit juice.
additives.smallDose: Labelled as an allergen in the EU above 10 mg/kg.
additives.largeDose: Can trigger asthma attacks in sulfite-sensitive people; destroys thiamin (vitamin B1).

additives.name: Sodium metabisulfite
additives.what: Sulfite-releasing preservative common in wines, dried fruits, shellfish.
additives.smallDose: Required to be disclosed as an allergen in the EU.
additives.largeDose: A known trigger for asthma attacks in sulfite-sensitive people.

additives.name: Potassium nitrite
additives.what: Preservative and color fixative for cured meats. Prevents botulism.
additives.smallDose: Occasional intake unlikely to cause harm for most adults.
additives.largeDose: Can form N-nitroso compounds during cooking, classified by IARC as 'probably carcinogenic'. WHO links processed meats to colorectal cancer.

additives.name: Sodium nitrite
additives.what: Used in bacon, ham, hot dogs to prevent botulism and keep the pink color.
additives.smallDose: Occasional consumption fits EU limits.
additives.largeDose: Processed meats cured with nitrites are Group 1 carcinogens (IARC). Regular daily consumption is linked to colorectal cancer.

additives.name: Sodium nitrate
additives.what: Converts to nitrite in the body; used in cured meats.
additives.smallDose: Permitted within strict limits.
additives.largeDose: Shares the nitrosamine and colorectal-cancer concerns of nitrites.

additives.name: Potassium nitrate
additives.what: Curing salt used in charcuterie.
additives.smallDose: Permitted within strict limits.
additives.largeDose: Same nitrosamine-forming concerns as E251.

additives.name: Lactic acid
additives.what: Natural acid produced in fermentation; present in yogurt and sourdough.
additives.smallDose: Safe.
additives.largeDose: No significant concerns.

additives.name: Propionic acid
additives.what: Mold inhibitor used in breads and baked goods.
additives.smallDose: Generally well tolerated.
additives.largeDose: Some animal studies suggest effects on insulin signalling at high intakes; EFSA's review concluded it's safe at current use levels.

additives.name: Ascorbic acid (vitamin C)
additives.what: Vitamin C, added as antioxidant to prevent browning and extend shelf life.
additives.smallDose: Safe and nutritionally useful.
additives.largeDose: Doses above a few grams per day can cause diarrhea. In drinks containing benzoate (E211), can form traces of benzene.

additives.name: Tocopherols (vitamin E)
additives.what: Natural vitamin E extract; antioxidant that stops fats going rancid.
additives.smallDose: Safe and nutritionally useful.
additives.largeDose: No significant concerns at food-additive levels.

additives.name: BHA (butylated hydroxyanisole)
additives.what: Synthetic antioxidant for fats and oils.
additives.smallDose: Approved within EU limits.
additives.largeDose: Classified by IARC as 'possibly carcinogenic' based on animal studies. Replaced by tocopherols in many products.

additives.name: BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene)
additives.what: Synthetic antioxidant similar to BHA.
additives.smallDose: Approved within EU limits.
additives.largeDose: Animal data show endocrine and tumor effects at high doses; long-term human evidence is limited.

additives.name: Lecithins
additives.what: Emulsifier extracted from soy, sunflower, or egg. Helps mix fat and water.
additives.smallDose: Safe. Soy lecithin may carry traces of soy protein relevant to severe soy allergy.
additives.largeDose: No significant concerns.

additives.name: Citric acid
additives.what: Naturally present in citrus fruit; industrially made by fermenting sugar with Aspergillus niger.
additives.smallDose: Safe.
additives.largeDose: Can erode tooth enamel in frequent acidic-drink consumers.

additives.name: Phosphoric acid
additives.what: Gives colas their sharp tang.
additives.smallDose: Occasional intake fine for most adults.
additives.largeDose: Heavy cola intake is linked to lower bone density and kidney-function decline. People with chronic kidney disease should limit phosphate intake.

additives.name: Carrageenan
additives.what: Seaweed-derived thickener used in dairy, plant milks, processed meats.
additives.smallDose: Food-grade carrageenan is approved by EFSA.
additives.largeDose: Some people with IBS or inflammatory bowel disease report symptoms with regular intake; evidence is mixed but many sensitive guts avoid it.

additives.name: Locust bean gum
additives.what: Natural thickener from carob tree seeds.
additives.smallDose: Safe.
additives.largeDose: Large amounts of any gum can cause bloating or loose stools.

additives.name: Guar gum
additives.what: Soluble fiber thickener from guar beans.
additives.smallDose: Safe; may slightly improve satiety.
additives.largeDose: At large amounts can cause gas or bloating.

additives.name: Xanthan gum
additives.what: Fermentation-derived thickener; common in gluten-free baking.
additives.smallDose: Safe for adults.
additives.largeDose: Avoid in infant food — linked to serious gut complications in newborns.

additives.name: Sorbitol
additives.what: Sugar alcohol sweetener; about 60% the sweetness of sugar.
additives.smallDose: Does not spike blood sugar; tooth-friendly.
additives.largeDose: Above ~20 g/day can cause diarrhea and bloating. Warning label required in the EU.

additives.name: Pectin
additives.what: Natural fruit fiber used to set jams and jellies.
additives.smallDose: Safe; contributes soluble fiber.
additives.largeDose: Very large amounts can cause bloating.

additives.name: Diphosphates
additives.what: Phosphate salts used as leavening agents and in processed meats.
additives.smallDose: Within limits for healthy adults.
additives.largeDose: Added phosphates are absorbed much more efficiently than natural phosphorus. People with kidney disease should minimise them.

additives.name: Cellulose
additives.what: Plant fiber, often added as an anti-caking or bulking agent.
additives.smallDose: Safe; passes through as insoluble fiber.
additives.largeDose: No significant concerns.

additives.name: Mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids
additives.what: Very common emulsifier in baked goods, ice cream, margarine.
additives.smallDose: Approved; source fat can be animal or plant.
additives.largeDose: Emerging research suggests frequent emulsifier intake may affect the gut microbiome. Evidence is preliminary.

additives.name: DATEM (mono/diglycerides esters)
additives.what: Dough conditioner widely used in industrial bread.
additives.smallDose: Approved at normal use levels.
additives.largeDose: Same emulsifier/microbiome caveat as E471.

additives.name: Sodium carbonates
additives.what: Baking soda and washing soda; leavening and acidity control.
additives.smallDose: Safe at food-additive levels.
additives.largeDose: Contributes to dietary sodium — relevant for blood pressure.

additives.name: Monosodium glutamate (MSG)
additives.what: Sodium salt of glutamic acid; produces umami (savory) taste.
additives.smallDose: EFSA considers it safe; classic 'Chinese restaurant syndrome' reports have not held up in controlled trials for most people.
additives.largeDose: Adds meaningful sodium; a minority report headache or flushing at high intakes.

additives.name: Disodium guanylate
additives.what: Umami enhancer, usually paired with MSG.
additives.smallDose: Safe.
additives.largeDose: Should be avoided by people with gout — metabolises to purines.

additives.name: Disodium inosinate
additives.what: Flavour enhancer often from fish or meat; used with MSG.
additives.smallDose: Safe.
additives.largeDose: People with gout should limit purine-producing additives.

additives.name: Acesulfame K
additives.what: Zero-calorie artificial sweetener, 200× sweeter than sugar.
additives.smallDose: EFSA considers it safe at the accepted daily intake.
additives.largeDose: Recent observational studies have linked high intakes of several non-nutritive sweeteners to a modest increase in cardiovascular events; evidence is evolving.

additives.name: Aspartame
additives.what: Low-calorie sweetener made of two amino acids. Must be declared for people with PKU.
additives.smallDose: EFSA keeps it within ADI. IARC (2023) classified it as 'possibly carcinogenic to humans' (Group 2B) — lowest risk category.
additives.largeDose: People with PKU must avoid it entirely (contains phenylalanine). Heavy daily diet-soda drinkers exceed the cautious intake in some studies.

additives.name: Cyclamate
additives.what: Artificial sweetener, banned in the US since 1970; still permitted in the EU.
additives.smallDose: EFSA's ADI is considered conservative.
additives.largeDose: Older bladder-cancer animal data remains debated. Heavy daily intake is uncommon.

additives.name: Saccharin
additives.what: One of the oldest artificial sweeteners.
additives.smallDose: Approved; no confirmed human cancer link.
additives.largeDose: Animal bladder-cancer signals led to earlier concerns; no clear human evidence.

additives.name: Sucralose
additives.what: Chlorinated sucrose; 600× sweeter than sugar.
additives.smallDose: Approved. Does not spike blood sugar in most studies.
additives.largeDose: Recent research suggests it may subtly alter gut microbiota and insulin response with regular daily use.

additives.name: Steviol glycosides (stevia)
additives.what: Sweetener from the stevia plant leaf.
additives.smallDose: Approved. Does not raise blood sugar. Generally considered the safest of non-sugar sweeteners.
additives.largeDose: Some people find a bitter aftertaste; no significant health concerns at current use.

additives.name: Maltitol
additives.what: Sugar alcohol sweetener; about 90% the sweetness of sugar.
additives.smallDose: Lower glycemic impact than sugar.
additives.largeDose: Above ~20 g/day causes laxative effects. EU label warning required.

additives.name: Erythritol
additives.what: Sugar alcohol; zero-calorie, almost no glycemic impact.
additives.smallDose: Well tolerated at typical food levels.
additives.largeDose: A 2023 study linked high blood levels to increased cardiovascular events; causality is debated.
